content.text: Discovery workshops produce insights, artefacts, and enthusiasm. Without a formal handoff gate, the transition from discovery output to sprint backlog is leaky: stories get created before data access is confirmed, engineers start coding before the AI Decision card specifies an eval metric, and compliance logging requirements surface mid-sprint when retrofitting them is expensive. The Discovery-to-Backlog Handoff Criteria define the minimum readiness bar that must be met before a sprint can be committed to.

attrs.alt: Discovery-to-Backlog Readiness Gate: three-column rubric (Not Ready / Needs Review / Ready) across six criteria: AI Decision Cards, Data access, Open questions, Hotspot spikes, Governance, and Bounded context

content.text: Why a Formal Gate Matters for GenAI Features

Standard Agile readiness criteria — stories have acceptance criteria, dependencies are identified, estimates are agreed — are necessary but not sufficient for GenAI features. Three categories of risk are unique to GenAI and are not captured by conventional 'definition of ready' checklists:
Model uncertainty.

An engineer cannot write an acceptance criterion for 'the model responds correctly' without knowing which model, at what temperature, against what evaluation harness, with what latency budget. Without an AI Decision Card, the acceptance criterion is effectively unmeasurable.
Data access blockers.

GenAI features typically depend on retrieval corpora, fine-tuning datasets, or real-time context APIs that have access, quality, or licence constraints. These constraints take longer to resolve than engineering work — a PII masking approach must be reviewed by a DPO; a third-party data licence must be reviewed by Legal. Discovering these blockers in sprint one causes two-week slips.
Governance retrofit cost.

Adding audit logging, content policy enforcement, and PII scrubbing after a feature is built is significantly more expensive than designing them in. The Governance Audit Event Cards must be agreed with Security and Platform before the first line of inference code is written.
The Six Handoff Criteria
Criterion 1: AI Decision Cards
Every purple AI Decision sticky from the event storm must have a corresponding AI Decision Card. At handoff time, the card must have:
A named model (or an explicit 'TBD — spike DS-NNN will decide by day 3 of sprint 1').
A defined input contract (format, max tokens, data sources the prompt draws from).
A defined output contract (schema, max length, fallback format on error).
A named primary evaluation metric (G-Eval faithfulness, accuracy@1, ROUGE, human CSAT, etc.) with a minimum threshold.
A specified fallback behaviour (error message to user, rule-based response, human escalation trigger).
Blocking condition:
If more than 25% of AI Decision Cards have both model_id and eval_harness as TBD at handoff, the sprint must not start. The team cannot estimate or test what they cannot define.
Criterion 2: Data Inventory Stub — Sprint-1 Sources Confirmed
Every data source needed for sprint-1 stories must have confirmed access. 'Confirmed access' means:
The team has a working API key, database credential, or S3 bucket access — not just a verbal confirmation that access 'should be available'.
The data schema has been inspected and is as expected (column names, types, sample record reviewed).
PII-bearing fields have been identified and the masking or pseudonymisation approach has been agreed (not just planned).
If a third-party data licence is involved, Legal has confirmed the licence permits the intended AI use.
Criterion 3: Open Questions — No Sprint-1 Blockers

The Open Questions Log from the workshop must be triaged against the sprint-1 backlog. Any question that blocks a sprint-1 story must have an owner and a committed answer-by date that falls within the first three days of the sprint. Questions that can be answered during the sprint but do not block any sprint-1 story may remain open — they are 'parallel track' questions, not handoff blockers.
Question Status
Blocks Sprint-1 Story?
Handoff Verdict
Open, has owner, answer-by day 1–3 of sprint
Yes
NEEDS REVIEW — acceptable, monitor daily
Open, has owner, answer-by day 4+
Yes
NOT READY — sprint cannot start; risk too high
Open, no owner
Yes or No
NOT READY — assign owner before handoff
Open, has owner, answer-by within sprint
No
READY — parallel track, not a blocker
Answered / Deferred with rationale
Any
READY — resolved or explicitly accepted as risk
Criterion 4: Hotspot Spikes — All Ticketed

Every red-dot hotspot from the event storm must exist as a time-boxed design spike ticket in the backlog. The ticket must have a definition of done and an assigned owner. Spikes that must be resolved before sprint-1 stories can start are 'gating spikes'; spikes that run in parallel are 'concurrent spikes'. Both are acceptable at handoff;
untracked hotspots are not
Criterion 5: Governance Audit Event Cards — Security-Approved

Every Governance swimlane event must have a completed Audit Event Card. The cards must be reviewed by the Security team and by a Platform engineer who can confirm the log destination exists and the SIEM alert is achievable before sprint-1 begins. Security review is not a sprint-1 story — it must precede sprint planning.
Common failure:

Teams treat governance as a post-launch checklist and start building the feature. Security review then discovers that the proposed log destination violates data residency rules, or that the log schema does not support the SIEM alert the compliance team needs. Retrofitting takes 2–3 weeks and often delays a production launch.
Criterion 6: Bounded Context Map — Team Ownership Assigned

Every bounded context identified during the event storm must have a named owning team. Contexts with contested or shared ownership must be resolved before sprint planning: split the context or assign a primary owner with an explicit interface contract to the secondary team.
Running the Handoff Gate

The handoff gate is a 60-minute meeting attended by the product owner, tech lead, an ML engineer, a data engineer, and the Security/Platform representative. It is not a workshop — it is a checklist review. The facilitator reads each criterion, the relevant owner confirms status, and the group votes: READY, NEEDS REVIEW, or NOT READY.

from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from enum import Enum

class GateStatus(str, Enum):
    READY        = "ready"
    NEEDS_REVIEW = "needs_review"
    NOT_READY    = "not_ready"

@dataclass
class HandoffCriterion:
    name: str
    owner: str
    status: GateStatus
    notes: str = ""

@dataclass
class HandoffGate:
    feature_name: str
    sprint_number: int
    criteria: list[HandoffCriterion] = field(default_factory=list)

    def overall_status(self) -> GateStatus:
        if any(c.status == GateStatus.NOT_READY for c in self.criteria):
            return GateStatus.NOT_READY
        if any(c.status == GateStatus.NEEDS_REVIEW for c in self.criteria):
            return GateStatus.NEEDS_REVIEW
        return GateStatus.READY

    def can_start_sprint(self) -> bool:
        return self.overall_status() != GateStatus.NOT_READY

    def blockers(self) -> list[HandoffCriterion]:
        return [c for c in self.criteria if c.status == GateStatus.NOT_READY]

    def report(self) -> str:
        lines = [f"Handoff Gate: {self.feature_name} — Sprint {self.sprint_number}"]
        lines.append(f"Overall: {self.overall_status().value.upper()}")
        lines.append(f"Sprint may start: {self.can_start_sprint()}")
        for c in self.criteria:
            icon = {"ready":"✓","needs_review":"⚠","not_ready":"✗"}[c.status.value]
            lines.append(f"  {icon} {c.name} [{c.owner}]: {c.notes or c.status.value}")
        return "\n".join(lines)

# Example gate evaluation
gate = HandoffGate("AI Customer Support Bot", sprint_number=1)
gate.criteria = [
    HandoffCriterion("AI Decision Cards",          "ml-lead",       GateStatus.NEEDS_REVIEW, "ResponseGenerated card: model TBD — spike DS-001 starts day 1"),
    HandoffCriterion("Data Inventory Stub",        "data-eng",      GateStatus.READY,        "All sprint-1 sources confirmed; PII masking agreed"),
    HandoffCriterion("Open Questions Log",         "product-owner", GateStatus.READY,        "2 open questions, both non-blocking for sprint-1"),
    HandoffCriterion("Hotspot Spikes",             "tech-lead",     GateStatus.READY,        "3 spikes ticketed; DS-001 is concurrent, not gating"),
    HandoffCriterion("Governance Audit Cards",     "security",      GateStatus.NOT_READY,    "ContentFlagged card pending Security review — ETA 2 days"),
    HandoffCriterion("Bounded Context Map",        "arch-lead",     GateStatus.READY,        "All 3 contexts have team owners"),
]
print(gate.report())
print(f"\nAction: Security review of ContentFlagged card must complete before sprint-1 planning.")
What Happens When the Gate Fails

A failed gate (any NOT READY criterion) means the sprint-1 planning meeting must be postponed until the blocking criterion is resolved. This is not a failure of the process — it is the process working correctly. The cost of a 2-day delay in sprint-1 planning is far lower than the cost of a 2-week mid-sprint rescope.
The protocol when a gate fails:
Identify the minimum set of actions
required to move the blocking criterion from NOT READY to NEEDS REVIEW or READY.
Assign each action to a named owner
with a specific date by which it must be complete. Dates must be business days, not sprint days.
Schedule a re-gate meeting
for the day after the latest action date. The re-gate covers only the previously-failing criteria — it does not re-evaluate criteria that already passed.
Communicate to the wider team
that sprint planning is postponed and the reason. Engineers can use the time to work on any pre-approved technical spikes or to participate in resolving the gate-blocking actions.
Anti-pattern:

Starting the sprint with a verbal agreement to 'fix the gate criteria in parallel'. This converts a discovery-phase risk into a sprint-phase risk, typically causing the same rework the gate was designed to prevent — just three weeks later and under more schedule pressure.
Tracking Handoff Gate History

Storing the gate outcome as a structured record in the repository creates a searchable audit trail and enables retrospective analysis of which criteria most often fail. This data drives improvements to the discovery workshop format over time.
Calibrating the Gate for Feature Size

Not every GenAI feature warrants the same gate rigour. A minor UX enhancement that adds a one-sentence AI-generated summary to an existing dashboard has a different risk profile than a full agentic workflow that makes decisions on behalf of users. The gate criteria scale with feature size and risk.
Feature Size
Characteristics
Gate Approach
Typical Gate Duration
Small (1–2 sprints)
Single AI Decision node; existing model; no new data sources; no novel compliance questions
Async written confirmation of all 6 criteria; no meeting required
1 business day
Medium (3–5 sprints)
2–5 AI Decision nodes; new retrieval corpus; standard compliance patterns
60-minute gate meeting with full team
2–3 business days including Security review
Large (6+ sprints) or novel (new modality, new data type, novel compliance territory)
Multiple bounded contexts; custom model; complex PII or cross-border flows; high-volume inference
Full gate meeting + 48h async review window for Security and Legal sign-off
5+ business days
Calibration guidance:

When in doubt, treat the feature as the next size up. The cost of over-gating a small feature (one extra meeting) is much lower than under-gating a large feature (mid-sprint regulatory surprise or data-access blocker).
Integration with Existing Agile Processes
The handoff gate complements rather than replaces existing Agile ceremonies. The mapping below shows how it integrates into a standard two-week sprint cycle:
Day
Activity
Relationship to Handoff Gate
Discovery workshop
Event Storming, problem brief
Produces the raw material: event map, hotspots, parking lot
D+1 to D+3
Post-workshop artefact authoring
AI Decision Cards, Data Inventory Stub, Audit Event Cards drafted
Security review of Audit Event Cards
Gate criterion 5 — must complete before gate meeting
Handoff Gate meeting
6-criteria review; READY / NEEDS REVIEW / NOT READY verdict
D+6 to D+7
Address NEEDS REVIEW items; re-gate if any NOT READY
Blocking criteria resolved; sprint planning confirmed
Sprint planning
Uses gate-approved backlog; AI Decision Cards become acceptance criteria
Sprint week 1
Development begins; any 'day 1–3 answer' open questions resolved in parallel
Non-blocking questions answered without halting sprint
Definition of Ready — GenAI Extension
The standard 'Definition of Ready' (DoR) for a user story can be extended with three GenAI-specific fields that reference the handoff gate artefacts:

## Definition of Ready (GenAI Extension)

A user story is ready for sprint planning when ALL of the following are true:

### Standard DoR
- [ ] Story is written in user-story format with clear acceptance criteria
- [ ] Story is estimated (story points or T-shirt size)
- [ ] Dependencies identified; no external blockers in sprint-1
- [ ] Designs or wireframes available (for UI-facing stories)

### GenAI DoR Extension
- [ ] If story involves an AI Decision: the corresponding AI Decision Card is complete
      (model_id, eval metric, fallback, latency budget all specified)
- [ ] All data sources for this story have confirmed access and PII handling agreed
      (referenced in Data Inventory Stub, status = CONFIRMED)
- [ ] If story emits a Governance event: the Audit Event Card is complete and
      Security-approved (log destination, SIEM alert, retention all specified)
- [ ] Any hotspot spikes that gate this story are either resolved (ADR written)
      or explicitly scheduled as sprint-1 concurrent spikes (with defined handoff point)
Common Patterns That Evade the Gate
Even with a formal gate, certain patterns consistently bypass the criteria and create mid-sprint problems. Recognising these patterns helps facilitators and tech leads close the gaps.
The 'We'll Use the Existing Model' Assumption

Teams often assume they can reuse a model already deployed for another feature, skipping the AI Decision Card on the grounds that 'it's the same model we already use'. This is incorrect: different prompts, different contexts, and different latency budgets require separate evaluation and separate acceptance criteria. Every AI Decision node in the event storm is a separate card, even if it uses the same underlying model.
The 'Data is in the Warehouse' Shortcut

Having data in the data warehouse does not mean it is ready for use in a GenAI feature. The warehouse may contain fields that must be masked before being included in a prompt, have refresh latency that makes it unsuitable for real-time context, or carry a data classification that restricts its use to specific services. Confirmed access for a GenAI feature means the data has been assessed specifically for prompt inclusion, not just for analyst querying.
The 'Compliance Can Be Added Later' Deferral

Security and compliance reviews for Audit Event Cards are the most commonly deferred gate criterion. The justification is usually 'we don't want to block the sprint over a logging format'. The consequence is typically a security review mid-sprint that identifies a log-schema mismatch with the SIEM, requiring a refactor of the event emission code. Making Security review a non-negotiable pre-sprint gate condition eliminates this pattern.

# Gate bypass detection: patterns that indicate a gate was not run properly
from dataclasses import dataclass

@dataclass
class GateBypassRisk:
    pattern: str
    detection: str
    remediation: str

KNOWN_BYPASS_PATTERNS = [
    GateBypassRisk(
        pattern="Reusing existing model without a new AI Decision Card",
        detection="Sprint-1 stories reference AI behaviour but no AI Decision Card exists in docs/",
        remediation="Require a card for every AI Decision node, even if model_id is already known. "
                    "Card captures eval metric and fallback, which change per use case.",
    ),
    GateBypassRisk(
        pattern="Data Inventory Stub lists sources as 'in the warehouse' without schema review",
        detection="access_status = PENDING for any sprint-1 source at planning time",
        remediation="Require that status = CONFIRMED with a date and the name of the engineer "
                    "who tested access and reviewed the schema.",
    ),
    GateBypassRisk(
        pattern="Audit Event Cards not reviewed by Security before sprint planning",
        detection="Gate history shows criterion 5 = NEEDS_REVIEW at sprint start",
        remediation="Book Security review slot as part of the post-workshop artefact authoring "
                    "schedule (day 4 in the standard timeline). Make it a calendar event, not an async request.",
    ),
    GateBypassRisk(
        pattern="Open question log not created; questions remain in Miro parking lot",
        detection="No open-questions.json (or equivalent) committed to the repository by day D+3",
        remediation="The facilitator is responsible for exporting the parking lot to the questions "
                    "log within 24 hours of the workshop. This is a process gate, not an async task.",
    ),
]

for risk in KNOWN_BYPASS_PATTERNS:
    print(f"Pattern: {risk.pattern}")
    print(f"  Detect: {risk.detection}")
    print(f"  Fix:    {risk.remediation}")
    print()
Summary
The Discovery-to-Backlog Handoff Gate converts workshop output into sprint-ready commitment. Key disciplines:
AI Decision Cards are the primary gate.
A sprint cannot deliver a GenAI feature if the model contract — input, output, eval metric, fallback — is undefined. This is not an engineering detail; it is a product commitment.
Data access must be tested, not assumed.
'We think we can get access' is not a handoff-ready data status. Only confirmed, schema-reviewed, PII-assessed access satisfies criterion 2.
Security review of audit cards must precede sprint planning.
Governance requirements cannot be designed in if they have not been agreed. Security review is a discovery-phase activity, not a sprint-1 activity.
A failed gate is not a failure.

Postponing sprint planning by 2 days to resolve a NOT READY criterion is the correct outcome. The alternative — starting the sprint with unresolved blockers — consistently produces larger delays and rework.
Record gate outcomes for continuous improvement.
Tracking which criteria most often fail drives targeted improvements to the discovery workshop format, the artefact templates, and the pre-workshop preparation process.
